runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 5 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012) 'Bounds on Elasticities With Optimization Frictions' - surveys compensated labor supply elasticities, finding typical values around 0.2–0.5 for prime-age workers
CBO (2012) 'Labor Supply and Fiscal Policy' - uses substitution elasticities around 0.25 for primary earners in microsimulation
Saez, Slemrod, Giertz (2012) 'The Elasticity of Taxable Income with Respect to Marginal Tax Rates' - reviews ETI literature with implications for labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 5 (near-median earners) are typically prime-age, full-time workers with relatively inelastic labor supply. The empirical literature (Chetty 2012, CBO microsimulation models) generally places compensated substitution elasticities for primary earners around 0.1–0.4, with a central tendency near 0.2–0.3. Decile 5 workers are not at the extremes where elasticities tend to be higher (e.g., very low earners near program phase-outs or very high earners). I center my belief at 0.25, reflecting the mainstream empirical consensus for middle-income primary earners, with moderate uncertainty given variation across studies and model specifications.

Decile 5 workers are not at the extremes where elasticities tend to be higher (e.g., very low earners near program phase-outs or very high earners). - Population/context: Primary earners in earnings decile 5 in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 5 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012, Annual Review of Economics) — survey of intensive-margin labor supply elasticities, central estimates ~0.33 for compensated elasticity
CBO (2012) labor supply model parameters — primary earner compensated elasticities in the 0.15–0.30 range for middle earners
Keane (2011, Journal of Economic Literature) — review of male labor supply elasticities, compensated estimates often 0.1–0.3
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 5 (near-median earners) are typically full-time workers with moderate responsiveness to net wages. The literature on compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners—especially men near the median—clusters in the 0.1–0.4 range. Microsimulation models (e.g., CBO's) tend to use values around 0.2–0.3 for primary earners in middle deciles. Secondary earners have much higher elasticities; primary earners lower. Decile 5 earners face moderate effective marginal tax rates under current law. I center my belief at 0.25, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05–0.50.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 5 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012) 'Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions', Econometrica – surveys compensated labor supply elasticities centering around 0.25–0.5 for primary earners
Keane (2011) 'Labor Supply and Taxes: A Survey', Journal of Economic Literature – reports primary earner elasticities typically 0.1–0.3
CBO (2012) 'Effective Marginal Tax Rates for Low- and Moderate-Income Workers' – documents effective marginal rates by decile relevant to microsimulation contexts
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically male household heads or the higher-earning spouse) are generally found to have relatively inelastic labor supply compared to secondary earners. The literature consensus for compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners clusters between 0.1 and 0.5, with many estimates near 0.2–0.3. Decile 5 represents middle-income workers facing moderate effective marginal tax rates under U.S. current law. In microsimulation models (e.g., Tax-Calculator, TAXSIM-based models), the assigned substitution elasticity for primary earners in the middle deciles is commonly set around 0.25, consistent with mainstream empirical estimates. Uncertainty is moderate: the true value could plausibly range from ~0.10 (very inelastic, consistent with newer quasi-experimental estimates) to ~0.45 (higher end from older structural estimates).

citations: Chetty, R. (2012). Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions. Econometrica.
CBO (2012). Labor Supply and Fiscal Policy. Congressional Budget Office.
Saez, E., Slemrod, J., & Giertz, S. (2012). The Elasticity of Taxable Income with Respect to Marginal Tax Rates. Journal of Economic Literature.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically heads of household with significant labor market attachment) exhibit lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. The literature consensus places compensated wage elasticities for primary earners in the range of roughly 0.1–0.4, with most point estimates near 0.2–0.3. For decile 5 (median earnings range), these workers face moderate effective marginal tax rates and are in the middle of the labor supply responsiveness distribution—neither the very low earners (who may face high implicit marginal rates via phase-outs) nor high earners. In standard U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., those using CBO or Tax Policy Center assumptions), the substitution elasticity for primary earners in the middle deciles is typically calibrated around 0.25. Uncertainty remains about the exact value used in any specific model, so I assign a moderately wide credible interval of 0.05–0.50.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the middle of the earnings distribution (decile 5) are generally found to have moderate compensated (substitution) elasticities. The mainstream labor-supply literature places compensated wage elasticities for prime-age male and mixed primary earners in the range of roughly 0.1 to 0.4, with a central tendency near 0.2–0.3. Tax-benefit microsimulation models (e.g., those used by CBO or Urban-Brookings) typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities around 0.25 for middle-income workers. Decile 5 sits at median earnings where income effects are modest and labor supply is relatively inelastic compared to secondary earners, supporting a central estimate of 0.25 with meaningful uncertainty on both sides.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 5 (near the median of the earnings distribution) are typically prime-age workers, often male household heads, for whom the empirical labor-supply literature finds relatively modest compensated (substitution) elasticities. Tax-benefit microsimulation models (e.g., those used by CBO or Tax Policy Center) tend to calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities near 0.2–0.3. I center my estimate at 0.25 with meaningful uncertainty reflecting both model variation and empirical heterogeneity.

Decile 5 represents middle-income workers who face typical effective marginal tax rates and are less likely to be at extensive-margin kinks. Tax-benefit microsimulation models (e.g., those used by CBO or Tax Policy Center) tend to calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities near 0.2\u20130.3. I center my estimate at 0.25 with meaningful uncertainty reflecting both model variation and empirical heterogeneity."}
